questionBank.question: What is the average value of f(x) = x² on the interval [0, 3]?
questionBank.notesSnippet: Average value of f on [a,b] = 1/(b−a)·∫ₐᵇf(x)dx. Divide the integral by the length of the interval.
questionBank.timeTip: Don't forget to divide by (b−a)! That's the most common mistake. Average value = integral ÷ interval length.

questionBank.question: The average value of g(x) = 2x + 1 on [1, 4] is:
questionBank.notesSnippet: Average value = 1/(b−a)·∫ₐᵇf(x)dx. For linear functions, the average value equals f evaluated at the midpoint.
questionBank.timeTip: Quick check for linear functions: average value = f((a+b)/2). Here f(2.5) = 2(2.5)+1 = 6 ✓. Use this to verify your answer.

questionBank.question: If the average value of f on [1, 5] is 8, what is ∫₁⁵ f(x) dx?
questionBank.notesSnippet: If average = k, then integral = k·(b−a). Work backwards: integral = average value × interval length.
questionBank.timeTip: Work backwards: integral = average × length. The AP exam tests this direction too. Know both: average = integral/length AND integral = average × length.

questionBank.question: A particle moves along the x-axis with velocity v(t) = t² − 4t + 3. What is the displacement from t = 0 to t = 3?
questionBank.notesSnippet: Displacement = ∫v(t)dt (signed). The particle ends where it started if displacement = 0, even if it moved around.
questionBank.timeTip: Displacement = signed integral of velocity. The particle here went out and came back, ending at start. Don't confuse with total distance traveled.

questionBank.question: For v(t) = t² − 4t + 3 on [0, 3], the total distance traveled is:
questionBank.notesSnippet: Total distance = ∫|v(t)|dt. Find where v(t)=0, split integral there, and add absolute values of each piece.
questionBank.timeTip: Total distance ≠ displacement when particle reverses. Find zeros of v(t), integrate each piece, add absolute values. Show this work on the AP FRQ for full credit.

questionBank.question: A particle has x(0) = 5 and v(t) = 2t + 1. Find x(3).
questionBank.notesSnippet: x(b) = x(a) + ∫ₐᵇv(t)dt. Start from the initial position and add the accumulated change from integrating velocity.
questionBank.timeTip: Position = initial + integral of velocity. This is the Net Change Theorem. Always start from the given initial condition, don't forget it!

questionBank.question: Water flows into a tank at r(t) = 4t + 2 gal/min. The tank has 10 gallons at t = 0. How many gallons at t = 3?
questionBank.notesSnippet: f(b) = f(a) + ∫ₐᵇf'(t)dt. Starting amount + integral of rate = final amount. Check units: gal/min × min = gal.
questionBank.timeTip: Label units every time. Rate × time = amount. Don't forget to add the initial value — a very common AP error.

questionBank.question: Oil leaks at rate L(t) gal/hr. Which gives total oil leaked from t = 2 to t = 6?
questionBank.explanation: Total amount leaked = integral of the rate over the interval = ∫₂⁶L(t)dt.
questionBank.notesSnippet: ∫rate dt = net change in quantity. If L(t) is gal/hr, ∫L(t)dt gives gallons. Rate integrated = accumulated amount.
questionBank.timeTip: Words like "total," "accumulated," or "how much" over a time interval → you need an integral of the rate, not just a function value.

questionBank.question: Find the area between f(x) = x² and g(x) = x on [0, 1].
questionBank.notesSnippet: Area = ∫ₐᵇ[top−bottom]dx. Always subtract the lower curve from the upper curve. Test a point to confirm which is on top.
questionBank.timeTip: Test x=0.5: g(0.5)=0.5, f(0.5)=0.25 → g is on top. Integrate (g−f) = (x−x²). Don't skip the "which is on top" check!

questionBank.question: Find the area enclosed by y = x² and y = 2x.
questionBank.explanation: Set equal: x²=2x → x=0,2. On [0,2]: 2x ≥ x². Area = ∫₀²(2x−x²)dx = [x²−x³/3]₀² = 4−8/3 = 4/3.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Set curves equal to find limits of integration. x²=2x → x(x−2)=0 → x=0,2. Then integrate (top−bottom) between those bounds.
questionBank.timeTip: Always find intersections first — they give your limits of integration. Set the two functions equal and solve. This is a must-show step on the AP FRQ.

questionBank.question: The area between f(x) = 4 and g(x) = x² on [−2, 2] is:
questionBank.notesSnippet: Area = ∫(top−bottom)dx. Simplify the difference algebraically first, then integrate. For symmetric regions around y-axis, use 2·∫₀ᵇ.

questionBank.question: A solid's base is bounded by y = √x and y = 0 on [0, 4]. Cross sections perpendicular to the x-axis are squares. Find the volume.
questionBank.notesSnippet: V = ∫A(x)dx. For squares: A = s². Find s from the bounding curves (s = top − bottom), then integrate the area formula.
questionBank.timeTip: Step 1: s = top curve − bottom curve. Step 2: A(x) = s² for squares. Step 3: integrate. Always identify the cross-section shape and its area formula first.

questionBank.question: A solid has base bounded by x = 0, x = 4, y = 0, y = 2. Cross sections perpendicular to the x-axis are right isosceles triangles with legs in the base. What is the volume?
questionBank.explanation: The base of each triangle has length 2 (height of the region). For a right isosceles triangle with leg s: A = s²/2. Here s=2, so A=2. V = ∫₀⁴2 dx = 8.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Right isosceles triangle (legs = s): A = s²/2. Equilateral triangle (side = s): A = (√3/4)s². Semicircle (diameter = s): A = πs²/8.
questionBank.timeTip: Memorize cross-section area formulas: square s², rectangle s·w, right isosceles s²/2, equilateral (√3/4)s², semicircle πs²/8. These are the only shapes the AP tests.

questionBank.question: Find the volume when y = √x on [0, 4] is revolved about the x-axis.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Disc method (around x-axis): V = π∫ₐᵇ[f(x)]²dx. Radius = f(x). Square it first, then integrate, then multiply by π.
questionBank.timeTip: Disc = πr². Radius = function value. V = π∫r²dx. The π goes outside the integral. Square the function BEFORE integrating — a very common error.

questionBank.question: Which integral gives the volume when y = x² on [0, 2] is revolved about the x-axis?
questionBank.explanation: Disc method: V = π∫₀²(x²)²dx = π∫₀²x⁴dx. The radius is x², and we square it to get x⁴.
questionBank.notesSnippet: V = π∫[f(x)]²dx. Square the entire function. Here (x²)² = x⁴, not x².
questionBank.timeTip: (x²)² = x⁴, not 2x². Squaring a power multiplies the exponents: (xⁿ)² = x²ⁿ. Write out the squaring step explicitly.

questionBank.question: The region between y = x and y = x² on [0,1] is revolved about the x-axis. Find the volume.
questionBank.explanation: R = x (outer), r = x² (inner). V = π∫₀¹[(x)²−(x²)²]dx = π∫₀¹[x²−x⁴]dx = π[x³/3−x⁵/5]₀¹ = π(1/3−1/5) = 2π/15.
questionBank.notesSnippet: V = π∫[R²−r²]dx. R = outer radius (top curve when rotating around x-axis). r = inner radius (bottom curve). R²−r² ≠ (R−r)²!
questionBank.timeTip: Biggest AP mistake: (R−r)² ≠ R²−r². You MUST square R and r separately, then subtract. Write π∫(R²−r²)dx and fill in R and r explicitly.

questionBank.question: When is the washer method needed instead of the disc method?

choices.A: When rotating around the y-axis instead of x-axis
choices.B: When the region does not touch the axis of rotation, creating a hole
choices.C: When the function is not continuous
choices.D: When rotating about a line y = k

questionBank.explanation: The washer method is needed when there is a hole — i.e., the region doesn't touch the axis of rotation. If the region includes the axis, use the disc method.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Disc: region touches the axis (no hole). Washer: region doesn't touch axis (ring-shaped cross section). The "hole" creates the inner radius r.
questionBank.timeTip: Quick check: does the region touch the axis of rotation? Yes → disc. No → washer. Draw a picture to see if there's a hole.

parts.label: (1a)
parts.question: Set up an expression or equation for each of the following questions. At time 푡=0 hours, there are 213 cars in the parking lot of a large apartment complex. For 0≤푡 ≤7 hours, the rate that cars are entering the parking lot can be modeled by the function 퐸(푡), measured in cars per hour. During this same time period, cars leave the parking lot at the rate 퐿(푡), measured in cars per hour. a) How many cars leave the parking lot during the first 4 hours? b) How many cars enter the parking lot from time 푡 =1 to 푡 =6 hours? c) How many cars are in the parking lot at time 푡 =5? d) Write an equation involving one or more integrals for 퐴(푡), the number of cars in the parking lot at any time 푡, for 0≤푡 ≤7.

Due to a company policy, from 7AM to 9AM no employees leave the office building. Starting at 9AM, employees exit the building at a rate modeled by 푓 ( 푡 ) =97−81cos ൬ 휋푡−2푡 2.9 ൰ for 2≤푡 ≤10 where 푓 ( 푡 ) is measured in people per hour and 푡 is the number of hours since 7AM. a) How many employees entered the building from 7AM to 9AM? b) How many employees have left the building by 3PM? c) Write an equation for 푃(푡) where 푃(푡) gives the number of employees in the building at any time 푡 for 2≤푡 ≤10.

parts.question: The number of trees that are planted in a large forest is changing at the rate 푝 ᇱ ( 푡 ) =−0.2 ( 푡−11 ) ଶ +131 In the same forest, trees are being cut down at the rate 푐 ᇱ ( 푡 ) =37 ln (0.84푡+3.8) where 푐′(푡) and 푝 ᇱ ( 푡 ) are both measured in trees per day and 푡 is measured in days. There are 1200 trees in the forest at time 푡 =0 days. a) How many trees are planted during the first 15 days? b) How many trees are in the forest after 30 days?

parts.label: (1a)
parts.question: A small car dealership in Plugtown has 29 cars on the lot at time 푡=0. During the time interval 0≤푡≤20 days, new cars are added to the lot at the rate 푁 ( 푡 ) =−0.018푡 ଶ +11 cars per day During the same time interval, cars are sold and removed from the lot at the rate 푆 ( 푡 ) =0.013푡 ଶ −0.25푡+8 cars per day (a) Is the amount of cars on the lot increasing or decreasing at time 푡=11? Explain your reasoning. (b) To the nearest whole number, how many cars are on the lot at time 푡=20? (c) At what time 푡, for 0≤푡≤20, is the number of cars on the lot a maximum? Justify your answer. (d) If the dealership has less than 10 cars on the lot, the owner will penalize the manager of the lot. Set up, but do not solve, an inequality involving one or more integrals that could be used to find the time 푤 when the amount of cars on the lot is less than or equal to 10. Created by B Passwater
parts.finalAnswer: Use the answer key PDF in Materials for this worksheet.
steps: Show all steps on paper.
Confirm with the answer key PDF.

parts.label: (2a)
parts.question: A large tank contains 40 gallons of water at time 푡=0. During the time interval 0≤푡≤2 hours, water is being poured into the tank at the rate 푊 ( 푡 ) =0.6푒 ௧ మ ିଵ gallons per hour During the same time interval, water is leaking from a small hole that is being fixed. The rate that water leaks out is given by: 퐿 ( 푡 ) =6cos ( 0.7푡 ) gallons per hour (a) How much water has been poured into the tank after 2 hours? (b) Find the time 푡, on 0≤푡≤2 hours, when the amount of water in the tank is a minimum. Justify your answer. (c) Is the amount of water in the tank increasing or decreasing at 푡=1? Give a reason for your answer. (d) The tank holds up to 300 gallons of water. For 푡>2, water continues to be poured into the tank at a rate of 푊(푡). However, water leaks out of the tank at a constant rate of 1.02 gallons per hour. Set up, but do not evaluate, an equation involving one or more integrals that could be used to find the time 푤 when the tank first becomes full. Created by B Passwater
parts.finalAnswer: Use the answer key PDF in Materials for this worksheet.
steps: Show all steps on paper.

parts.label: (3a)
parts.question: The number of a particular species of rabbits, in hundreds, in a wooded area is modeled by a differentiable function 푅 of time 푡, where 푅(푡) is the number of rabbits, in hundreds, and 푡 is measured in months, for 0≤푡≤50. There are 1500 rabbits in the wooded area at time 푡=0. The birth rate for the rabbits in the wooded area is modeled by 퐵 ( 푡 ) =1.3ln ( 푡+1 ) hundreds of rabbits per month and the death rate for the rabbits in the wooded area is modeled by 퐷 ( 푡 ) = 5 1+52푒 ି଴.ଷ௧ − 5 53 hundreds of rabbits per month (a) Is the population of rabbits in the wooded area increasing or decreasing at time 푡=10? Explain your reasoning. (b) To the nearest whole number, what is the population of rabbits in the wooded area at time 푡=50? (c) To the nearest whole number, determine the maximum and minimum population of rabbits in the wooded area for 0≤푡≤50. Justify your answer. (d) On what interval(s), for 0≤푡≤50, is the population of rabbits in the wooded area increasing? Give a reason for your answer. Created by B Passwater
parts.finalAnswer: Use the answer key PDF in Materials for this worksheet.
steps: Show all steps on paper.
Confirm with the answer key PDF.

parts.label: (4a)
parts.question: A parking garage has 230 cars in it when it opens at 8퐴푀 (푡=0). On the interval 0≤푡≤10, cars enter the parking garage at the rate 퐸 ᇱ ( 푡 ) =58cos ( 0.1635푡−0.642 ) cars per hour and cars leave the parking garage at the rate 퐿 ᇱ ( 푡 ) =65sin ( 0.281푡 ) +7.1 cars per hour (a) How many cars enter the parking garage over the interval 푡=0 to 푡=10 hours? (b) Find 퐸′′(5). Using correct units, explaining the meaning of this value in context of the problem. (c) Find the number of cars in the parking garage at time 푡=10. Show the work that leads to your answer. (d) Find the time 푡 on 0≤푡≤10, when the number of cars in the parking garage is a maximum. frqBank.worksheetPdf: Unit 8/Homework/8-9 to 8-12 WK B Volumes Disc & Washer #1.pdf

8.1.title: 8-1: Average Value of a Function
8.1.content:
CHA-4.B.1 · Average Value

Average Value Formula

The average value of a continuous function f on [a, b] is:

Ave Value = 1/(b − a) · ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx

Think of it as: divide the integral by the length of the interval. This gives the "average height" of the function.

AP Note: Don't forget the 1/(b−a) factor! That is the most common mistake on this problem type.

Mean Value Theorem for Integrals

If f is continuous on [a, b], then there exists at least one c in [a, b] such that f(c) = average value of f on [a, b]. In other words, the function actually reaches its average value somewhere on the interval.

AP Note: If asked to find c where f(c) = average value, solve f(c) = (1/(b−a))∫ₐᵇf(x)dx for c.

8.2.title: 8-2 & 8-3: Particle Motion and Applied Contexts
8.2.content:
CHA-4.D · Particle Motion

Position, Velocity, Acceleration

  • Position: x(t) — where the particle is
  • Velocity: v(t) = x'(t) — rate of change of position
  • Acceleration: a(t) = v'(t) — rate of change of velocity
x(b) = x(a) + ∫ₐᵇ v(t) dt   (Net Change Theorem)

Displacement vs. Total Distance

Displacement (signed)

= ∫ₐᵇ v(t) dt   Can be negative if particle moves backward

Total Distance (always positive)

= ∫ₐᵇ |v(t)| dt   Find where v = 0, split integral, add absolute values

AP Note: Displacement vs. total distance is tested EVERY year. Always find where v(t) = 0 and check sign changes before setting up distance.

Net Change Theorem in Context

For any quantity Q with rate of change Q'(t):

Q(b) = Q(a) + ∫ₐᵇ Q'(t) dt

Starting value + accumulated change = final value. Units: rate × time = quantity.

8.4.title: 8-4 to 8-6: Area Between Curves
8.4.content:
CHA-5.A · Area Between Curves

Area Between Two Curves

Area = ∫ₐᵇ [top − bottom] dx = ∫ₐᵇ [f(x) − g(x)] dx

Two things you need: (1) Where do the curves intersect? (set f = g and solve for bounds). (2) Which curve is on top? (test a point in the interval).

AP Note: Setting curves equal to find intersection points is almost always Step 1. Show this work explicitly on the AP FRQ.

When Functions Switch Position

If the curves cross within the interval, split the integral at the crossing point:

Area = ∫ₐᶜ [f−g] dx + ∫ᶜᵇ [g−f] dx   (where f and g switch at x = c)

Area with Respect to y

Sometimes it's easier to integrate with respect to y (horizontal slices):

Area = ∫ᶜᵈ [right − left] dy

8.7.title: 8-7 & 8-8: Volumes with Known Cross Sections
8.7.content:
CHA-5.B · Cross Section Volumes

General Volume Formula

V = ∫ₐᵇ A(x) dx   or   V = ∫ᶜᵈ A(y) dy

A(x) = area of the cross section at position x. The bounded region is the BASE of the solid; cross sections rise up perpendicularly.

Cross Section Area Formulas

Let s = side length = distance between bounding curves at position x.

SquareA = s²
Rectangle (height h)A = s · h
Right isosceles triangle (leg = s)A = s²/2
Equilateral triangle (side = s)A = (√3/4)s²
Semicircle (diameter = s)A = π(s/2)²/2 = πs²/8
AP Note: Memorize these formulas. The AP exam almost always uses squares, right isosceles triangles, or semicircles.

8.9.title: 8-9 & 8-10: Volume — Disc Method
8.9.content:
CHA-5.C · Disc Method

Disc Method (Rotating Around an Axis)

When a region is rotated around the x-axis, each cross section is a circle (disc). The radius of each disc = the function value.

V = π ∫ₐᵇ [f(x)]² dx   (rotation around x-axis)
V = π ∫ᶜᵈ [g(y)]² dy   (rotation around y-axis)
AP Note: Don't forget to square the function BEFORE integrating. A very common error is integrating f(x) and then squaring.

Rotation Around Non-Standard Axes

When rotating around y = k (not the x-axis), the radius changes:

Radius = |f(x) − k|

If the axis is below the region (k < 0), the radius is f(x) − k, which adds to the distance. Draw a picture to identify the radius.

8.11.title: 8-11 & 8-12: Volume — Washer Method
8.11.content:
CHA-5.C · Washer Method

Washer Method

Use when the region does NOT touch the axis of rotation, creating a "hole" (washer shape). R = outer radius, r = inner radius.

V = π ∫ₐᵇ [R(x)² − r(x)²] dx
Critical Warning

R² − r² ≠ (R − r)². You MUST square each radius separately, then subtract!

AP Note: The #1 AP error on washer problems is computing (R−r)² instead of R²−r². Write out R and r clearly before squaring.

Disc vs. Washer — How to Decide

  • Disc: The region touches the axis of rotation (no hole). One function, V = π∫f²dx.
  • Washer: The region does NOT touch the axis (has a hole). Two functions, V = π∫(R²−r²)dx.

Quick test: draw the region and the axis. If the axis passes through the region, it's a disc. If there's a gap between axis and region, it's a washer.

referenceHtml:
Unit 8 — Materials on this site include 5 notes PDFs, 12 homework PDFs, and 0 review PDFs. Copy your course PDFs into the project's materials/ folder, keeping paths like Unit 2/Homework/… so FRQ iframes and Packet PDF links load.

The formula blocks below are the shared AP Calc AB reference; your unit packets explain how they show up in your course.
referenceBlurb: Shared AB formulas plus a short pointer to this unit’s PDF materials.
